one_line_takeaway: AgentWatcher pairs attention-sink context attribution with an explicit-rule monitor LLM to detect prompt injection in long-context agents, cutting ASR to near zero across AgentDojo, InjecAgent, WASP, AgentDyn, and six LongBench datasets with minimal utility loss.
key_finding: AgentWatcher achieves the best utility-robustness trade-off among all compared detectors. On AgentDojo it cuts ASR to <=1% (0.01 important-instructions, 0.0 tool-call) with only ~2% utility loss (0.71 vs 0.73 no-defense), and it is the only method that keeps ASR at most 10% across ALL agent and long-context settings. It generalizes across backbone LLMs (Qwen-3-4B, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1-mini, Claude-Haiku-3, Gemini-2.0/2.5-Flash), driving ASR to near zero with <=4% utility loss. On the AgentDyn case study it is the only defense achieving simultaneously ASR 0.0% and relatively strong utility 48.3%. Automatically generated rules match hand-crafted rules; the attribution phase both lowers cost and improves accuracy vs. fixed-partition attribution baselines.
core_contribution: AgentWatcher is a detection-based defense against prompt injection in LLM agents, built from two of the authors' OWN phases. (1) An attribution phase that localizes the small span of untrusted context causally responsible for a risky agent action: it averages the backbone/attribution LLM's attention from context tokens to the action tokens over all heads/layers, uses a sliding window of size ws to find high-attention 'sink' tokens, and retains an expanded surrounding span (wl tokens left, wr right); this is repeated K times to get K non-overlapping windows whose concatenation is the attributed context C*. The novelty over prior attribution (ContextCite, TracLLM, AttnTrace, AT2) is that sink-token localization avoids the fixed-partition problem where an injected instruction gets split across segment boundaries. (2) A rule-based detection phase in which a monitor LLM reasons over C*, the (summarized) target task, and the agent's action against an explicit, customizable rule set R, emitting a reasoning trace plus a binary decision and, if positive, the extracted injected instruction. The authors further fine-tune the monitor with GRPO on 20,000 samples using a BLEU-based reward, and explore three strategies for automatically generating the rule set. Note: CaMeL, DRIFT, Progent, information-flow-control defenses, SecAlign, DataSentinel, AttentionTracker, etc. are cited baselines/related work, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against tool-using, web, and QA LLM agents. The agent f executes a user target task Iu over a multi-step trajectory, ingesting untrusted external contexts C1..Ct (emails, webpages, tool outputs). An attacker embeds one or more malicious instructions inside those contexts to divert the agent into an attacker-desired action (e.g., transferring money, exfiltrating data, visiting a URL). The defender's goal is a per-step binary detector Detector(St) that flags whether C1:t is contaminated and can halt execution/warn the user. The paper also evaluates adaptive attacks (heuristic and optimization-based) in the appendix.
limitations: Non-negligible latency: ~8.2s (stated elsewhere ~10s) per detection on a single A100 due to LLM reasoning, so the detector must be invoked selectively (blacklist of high-risk tool calls) rather than every step. Attribution over very long trajectories may need to be restricted to the most recent contexts to control cost. Achieving near-zero utility loss is inherently hard because benign instructions can resemble malicious ones. Effectiveness depends on the quality of the attention-based attribution and on the monitor LLM's reasoning; the defense is detection-only and is positioned as complementary to prevention-based defenses.

datasets_used: InjecAgent (indirect prompt injection, base and enhanced attacks)
AgentDyn (case study; shopping, GitHub, daily-life domains)
quantitative_results: AgentDojo AgentWatcher: Utility 0.71, ASR 0.01 (important-instructions), 0.0 (tool) vs No-Defense Utility 0.73, ASR 0.22/0.22
WASP AgentWatcher: Utility 0.99, ASR 0.02 vs No-Defense Utility 0.99, ASR 0.31

Long-context (Table 2, Qwen-3-4B monitor) AgentWatcher direct/combined ASR: LCC 0.03/0.02, GovReport 0.06/0.0, HotpotQA 0.0/0.0, MultiNews 0.05/0.0, PassageRetrieval 0.0/0.0, Qasper 0.04/0.0; clean utility matches or nearly matches no-defense
Only defense keeping ASR <=10% across all settings; <=4% utility loss across 7 backbone LLMs (Table 3)
AgentDyn (Fig 1, GPT-4o backbone): AgentWatcher ASR 0.0%, Utility 48.3% (only defense with both low ASR and strong utility)
Latency ~8.2s per detection on a single A100 GPU (~10s stated in discussion)
GRPO training set: 20,000 samples with BLEU-based reward; automatic rule sets fixed at |R|=10 (GPT-4o-mini generator)
Default hyperparameters: ws=10, wl=150, wr=50, K=3
baselines_compared: AgentDyn case study (9 baselines): Prompt Sandwich, Spotlighting, Tool Filter, ProtectAI, PIGuard, PromptGuard2, CaMeL, Progent, DRIFT
Attribution ablation baselines: log-probability, average attention, AT2, no-attribution
